question: Explain the 'heapify' procedure. Describe its purpose and walk through the steps it takes when called on a node that violates the max-heap property.
expectedPoints: Purpose: To maintain or restore the heap property for a subtree rooted at a given node.
Steps: Compare the node with its left and right children.
If a child is larger (in a max-heap), swap the node with the largest child.
Recursively call heapify on the subtree that was swapped into, which may now violate the heap property.

question: A common implementation of a priority queue uses a heap. Describe the steps involved in the 'extract-max' operation for a max-heap.
expectedPoints: The maximum element is always at the root of the max-heap.
Replace the root element with the last element in the heap.
Reduce the heap size by one.
Call the 'heapify' procedure on the new root to restore the max-heap property.

question: Compare and contrast the Heapsort algorithm with the Merge Sort algorithm. Your answer should cover their time complexity, space complexity, whether they are in-place, and their stability.
expectedPoints: Time Complexity: Both have an O(n log n) worst-case time complexity.
Space Complexity: Heapsort is O(1) (in-place), while Merge Sort requires O(n) auxiliary space.
In-place: Heapsort is an in-place algorithm; Merge Sort is not.
Stability: Heapsort is not stable due to the swapping operations in heapify. Merge sort is a stable algorithm.
